content.text: What the EU AI Act Is

The EU AI Act (Regulation (EU) 2024/1689) is the world's first comprehensive legal framework for artificial intelligence. Published in the Official Journal of the EU on 12 July 2024, it entered into force on 1 August 2024 and applies to any organisation that places AI systems on the EU market or uses AI systems that affect people in the EU — regardless of where the provider is established.
The Act uses a
risk-based approach
: obligations scale with potential harm. Four risk tiers determine what a provider, deployer, or importer must do before shipping.

attrs.alt: EU AI Act risk-based classification pyramid: Unacceptable, High, Limited, Minimal risk tiers plus GPAI cross-tier obligations

content.text: Risk Tier 1 — Prohibited AI Practices (Article 5)
The following AI practices are
banned outright
in the EU from 2 February 2025 (six months after entry into force):
Subliminal manipulation: techniques that exploit unconscious cues to impair autonomous decision-making.
Exploitation of vulnerabilities: targeting people based on age, disability, or socioeconomic situation.
Social scoring by public authorities: governmental AI that rates citizens' trustworthiness and denies rights based on the score.

Real-time remote biometric identification (RBI) in publicly accessible spaces by law enforcement — with narrow exceptions (missing children, imminent terror threat, post-hoc with judicial authorisation).
Emotion recognition in workplace and educational institutions (with narrow safety exceptions).
Biometric categorisation inferring sensitive attributes (race, political opinion, sexual orientation, religion) from biometric data.
AI-generated or manipulated content to create CSAM.

'Real-time' means without meaningful delay. Law enforcement post-hoc use of RBI for prosecution of serious crimes is permitted under strict conditions — prior judicial or independent authority authorisation required.
Risk Tier 2 — High-Risk AI Systems (Articles 8–49)
High-risk AI systems face the most extensive compliance obligations. A system is high-risk if it falls under
Annex I
(safety components of regulated products: medical devices, aviation, lifts, machinery) or
Annex III
(standalone high-risk use cases):
Annex III Area
Examples
Biometric identification
Real-time/post-hoc remote biometric ID; emotion recognition (non-prohibited cases)
Critical infrastructure
AI managing electricity, water, gas, road traffic
Education / vocational training
AI assigning exam scores, controlling access to education
Employment / HR
CV screening, promotion decisions, monitoring worker performance
Access to essential services
Creditworthiness, insurance pricing, social benefit eligibility
Law enforcement
Predictive policing, lie detection, evidence analysis
Migration / border control
Asylum applicant risk assessment, travel document verification
Administration of justice
AI assisting courts in legal research, fact-finding
Mandatory Obligations for High-Risk Systems
Providers of high-risk AI systems must, before placing them on the market:
Risk management system
(Article 9): documented lifecycle risk identification, estimation, evaluation, and mitigation.
Data governance
(Article 10): training/validation/test data quality, bias examination, relevance to intended purpose.
Technical documentation
(Article 11, Annex IV): 17-section dossier covering system description, data, training method, accuracy, robustness, cybersecurity.
Record-keeping / logging
(Article 12): automatic logging of operations, with logs retained per sector rules (minimum 6 months).
Transparency to deployers
(Article 13): instructions for use; clear statement of capabilities, limitations, foreseeable misuse.
Human oversight
(Article 14): technical measures enabling human operators to monitor, override, or halt the system.
Accuracy, robustness, cybersecurity
(Article 15): declared accuracy metrics; performance under input perturbation; resistance to adversarial attack.
Quality management system
(Article 17): documented processes covering design, testing, post-market monitoring, incident reporting.
Conformity assessment
(Article 43): self-assessment for most Annex III systems; mandatory third-party notified body for biometrics and law enforcement.
EU Declaration of Conformity
(Article 47) and
CE marking
(Article 48) before market placement.
Registration
(Article 49) in the EU AI database (high-risk standalone systems only).
Annex IV Technical Documentation — Key Sections

Annex IV Technical Documentation (17 sections, excerpt)

1.  General description of the AI system (name, version, intended purpose)
2.  Description of system components (algorithms, data, hardware)
3.  Description of the development process (design choices, training approach)
4.  Training and validation data: origin, collection methodology, labelling
5.  Performance metrics: accuracy, precision, recall, AUC, with confidence intervals
6.  Testing: test datasets, test results, worst-case scenarios
7.  Foreseeable unintended uses and misuse; measures to address them
8.  Human oversight measures and interfaces
9.  Cybersecurity: threat model, resistance testing results
10. Changes post-CE marking: version control, material change assessment
...
Risk Tier 3 — Limited Risk (Articles 50–52)
AI systems that interact with people or generate content face transparency obligations but no conformity assessment. The core obligations are:
Chatbots
: must inform users at the start of each interaction that they are talking to an AI, unless this is obvious from context.
Deepfakes
: providers of AI that generate synthetic audio, video, image, or text must label the content as AI-generated. Exempt if clearly creative or satirical and properly disclosed.
Emotion recognition / biometric categorisation
(non-prohibited cases): users must be informed before being subject to such a system.
Most conversational AI products (chatbots, virtual assistants, AI writing tools) fall here unless deployed in a high-risk context (HR, credit decisions, etc.).
Risk Tier 4 — Minimal Risk

The vast majority of AI systems — spam filters, recommendation engines, AI in games, image classification for personal use — carry no mandatory obligations under the Act. The European Commission encourages (but does not require) voluntary codes of conduct.
General Purpose AI (GPAI) Models — Title VIII

GPAI models (foundation models / large language models) are regulated separately because they cut across all risk tiers: the same model may power low-risk email summarisation and high-risk medical diagnosis. Obligations apply to model
providers
(not downstream deployers) from 2 August 2025 (12 months after entry into force).
Obligations for All GPAI Model Providers
Maintain technical documentation per Annex XI (architecture, training process, compute used, evaluation benchmarks).
Provide information and documentation to downstream deployers who build AI systems on top of the model.
Comply with EU copyright law (training data opt-out registers; licence documentation).
Publish a summary of training data — publicly, for transparency.
Systemic-Risk GPAI Models — Additional Obligations
A GPAI model has
systemic risk
if trained with more than 10^25 FLOPs (approximately GPT-4 scale and above, per recital 110), or if designated by the Commission based on capabilities assessment:
Adversarial testing (red-teaming) before model release, aligned with state-of-the-art protocols.
Incident reporting to the Commission: notify within 2 business days of any serious incident or discovered systemic risk.
Cybersecurity protection: physical security, access controls, model weight protection.
Energy efficiency reporting: training and inference power consumption.

10^25 FLOPs is a bright-line proxy, not a capability measure. The Commission has authority to designate smaller models as systemic-risk based on qualitative capability evaluation — this gives regulators flexibility as capability per FLOP improves over time.
GPAI Code of Practice

The AI Office (within the Commission) coordinates an industry code of practice for GPAI providers, co-developed with providers and civil society. Adhering to an approved code creates a rebuttable presumption of compliance. The first draft code was published in November 2024; finalisation expected mid-2025.
Governance and Enforcement
AI Office

A new AI Office within the European Commission has exclusive competence over GPAI models and coordinates enforcement across member states. It can conduct evaluations, request access to model weights, and impose fines.
National Competent Authorities

Each EU member state must designate one or more national competent authorities responsible for market surveillance and enforcement of the Act for AI systems (non-GPAI). Member states notified their designations by 2 August 2025.
Fines
Violation type
Maximum fine
Prohibited AI practices (Article 5)
€35 million or 7% of global annual turnover, whichever is higher
Other obligations (high-risk, GPAI)
€15 million or 3% of global annual turnover
Supplying incorrect or misleading information to authorities
€7.5 million or 1.5% of global annual turnover
SMEs / start-ups
Lower cap: the lower of the above figures or the absolute cap
Fines are per violation; multiple violations in a single product can accumulate.
Implementation Timeline
Date
Milestone
Act enters into force
Prohibited practices (Article 5) enforceable; AI literacy obligations (Article 4)
GPAI model obligations enforceable; AI Office fully operational; national authority designation
High-risk AI system obligations for Annex III systems enforceable; EU AI database live
Annex I high-risk systems (safety-component in regulated products) enforceable
Obligations for high-risk AI systems already on market before 2 Aug 2026 (transition period)
Compliance Tooling — Automating Risk Classification
The first practical step for any AI team is classifying each system by risk tier. The following classifier encodes the Act's decision tree:

from dataclasses import dataclass
from enum import Enum

class RiskTier(Enum):
    UNACCEPTABLE = "unacceptable"   # banned
    HIGH         = "high"           # full compliance required
    LIMITED      = "limited"        # transparency only
    MINIMAL      = "minimal"        # no obligations

@dataclass
class AISystem:
    name: str
    # Prohibited-practice flags (Article 5)
    uses_subliminal_manipulation: bool = False
    exploits_vulnerabilities: bool = False
    government_social_scoring: bool = False
    realtime_rbi_law_enforcement: bool = False
    workplace_emotion_recognition: bool = False
    biometric_categorisation_sensitive: bool = False
    # High-risk flags
    safety_component_regulated_product: bool = False  # Annex I
    annex_iii_use_case: str | None = None             # Annex III area or None
    # Limited-risk flags
    chatbot_or_avatar: bool = False
    generates_synthetic_content: bool = False
    emotion_recognition_non_prohibited: bool = False

    def classify(self) -> RiskTier:
        prohibited_flags = [
            self.uses_subliminal_manipulation,
            self.exploits_vulnerabilities,
            self.government_social_scoring,
            self.realtime_rbi_law_enforcement,
            self.workplace_emotion_recognition,
            self.biometric_categorisation_sensitive,
        ]
        if any(prohibited_flags):
            return RiskTier.UNACCEPTABLE
        if self.safety_component_regulated_product or self.annex_iii_use_case:
            return RiskTier.HIGH
        if any([self.chatbot_or_avatar,
                self.generates_synthetic_content,
                self.emotion_recognition_non_prohibited]):
            return RiskTier.LIMITED
        return RiskTier.MINIMAL

    def compliance_obligations(self) -> list[str]:
        tier = self.classify()
        if tier == RiskTier.UNACCEPTABLE:
            return ["PROHIBITED: this AI system must not be placed on the EU market"]
        if tier == RiskTier.HIGH:
            return [
                "Risk management system (Art. 9)",
                "Data governance (Art. 10)",
                "Technical documentation — Annex IV (Art. 11)",
                "Automatic logging (Art. 12)",
                "Transparency to deployers (Art. 13)",
                "Human oversight measures (Art. 14)",
                "Accuracy / robustness / cybersecurity (Art. 15)",
                "Quality management system (Art. 17)",
                "Conformity assessment (Art. 43)",
                "EU Declaration of Conformity (Art. 47)",
                "CE marking (Art. 48)",
                "Registration in EU AI database (Art. 49)",
            ]
        if tier == RiskTier.LIMITED:
            obligations = []
            if self.chatbot_or_avatar:
                obligations.append("Disclose AI nature to users at interaction start (Art. 50(1))")
            if self.generates_synthetic_content:
                obligations.append("Label synthetic content as AI-generated (Art. 50(2)-(4))")
            if self.emotion_recognition_non_prohibited:
                obligations.append("Inform users before subjecting them to emotion recognition (Art. 50(5))")
            return obligations
        return []  # minimal risk: no obligations

# Examples
systems = [
    AISystem("Customer chatbot", chatbot_or_avatar=True),
    AISystem("CV screening tool", annex_iii_use_case="employment"),
    AISystem("Deepfake video generator", generates_synthetic_content=True),
    AISystem("Social scoring app", government_social_scoring=True),
    AISystem("Spam filter"),
]

for s in systems:
    tier = s.classify()
    obls = s.compliance_obligations()
    print(f"\n{s.name}: {tier.value.upper()}")
    for o in obls[:3]:   # first 3 obligations for brevity
        print(f"  • {o}")
GPAI Model Card — Required Annex XII Information
Providers of GPAI models must publish an Annex XII summary containing at minimum:

# Annex XII — GPAI Model Summary (publicly required fields)

Model name and version: ___________________________
Provider and contact:   ___________________________
Release date:           ___________________________
Model type:             (text, image, multimodal, ...)
Intended purpose:       ___________________________

Training data:
  - Summary of sources (web, books, code, curated datasets)
  - Number of tokens / size of training set
  - Language coverage
  - Data collection time range
  - Known exclusions / opt-outs honoured

Compute:
  - Total training FLOPs (or training time + hardware)
  - Qualifies as systemic-risk (>10^25 FLOPs): YES / NO

Benchmark performance:
  - [Benchmark name]: [score] vs [baseline]
  ...

Known limitations and risks: ___________________________
Adversarial testing conducted: YES / NO
  If yes, methodology and summary of findings: ___________________________
Summary

The EU AI Act structures AI regulation around risk: prohibited systems are banned outright, high-risk systems require conformity assessment, CE marking, and a 12-section quality system, limited-risk systems need transparency labels, and minimal-risk systems face no obligations. GPAI models — foundation models and LLMs — have a parallel obligation track requiring technical documentation, training-data transparency, and (for systemic-risk models) adversarial testing and incident reporting. Enforcement begins in tiers: prohibited practices from February 2025, GPAI from August 2025, high-risk AI systems from August 2026. Fines reach 7% of global turnover for the most serious violations. The practical starting point for any team is a risk classification step — automatable with a decision-tree classifier — followed by a gap analysis against the relevant tier's obligation list.
